text:
<@UDKTZSD6H> I’m not even sure if I’m at a point yet where I should comment on art direction for a Christopher Alexander interpretation, but that Figma looks pretty amazing to me! I don’t understand how you can have such a good understanding of the properties without having read at least book 1 of Nature of Order?! I tried to piece it together from Internet resources before finally reading the book and I can now tell that I failed to grasp it. You don’t seem to have that same issue at all.

I will get in touch via DM about a call. Give me a few days… (I’m in a weird moving house situation that went a little sideways.)

text:
<@U5STGTB3J> Hey, thanks a lot for the kind words. Hope you sort out the house shifting issues and looking forward to talking with you soon.

Re: understanding strong centers, there’s this thread of philosophy from antiquity from time of Plato/Plotinus I could superimpose on Alexander’s philosophy. I also happen to read an article by him where he mentioned Spinoza, so I was trying to build something with that relation in mind.

elements.text:

Re: understanding strong centers, there’s this thread of philosophy from antiquity from time of Plato/Plotinus I could superimpose on Alexander’s philosophy. I also happen to read an article by him where he mentioned Spinoza, so I was trying to build something with that relation in mind.

text:
Hi!

Together with Damien Seguin and <@UEG9ECNSU> we have been building a node-based creative-coding tool at <http://variable.io> that is JavaScript based and npm friendly: Nodes.

We have used it for all our creative coding, generative art and datavis projects for the last 3 years and it’s really changed how we think with code. If you are interested in knowing more and trying it out, we just shared an open beta today for everyone: <https://twitter.com/nodes_io/status/1328666419890954241>

You can download it on our website: <https://nodes.io/>

--- end of announcement :slightly_smiling_face:

Happy to engage in any kind of discussion from “Why on earth JS?” to “Did you find unidirectional data flow useful or troublesome?” or even help getting started.

attachments.fallback:
<https://twitter.com/nodes_io|@nodes_io>: :sparkles:The open beta is here!:sparkles:

We are so proud to present the open beta of Nodes for you all to experiment and explore with. We hope you'll love this new way of creating with code.

Download here: <https://nodes.io> <https://pbs.twimg.com/ext_tw_video_thumb/1328666190659678209/pu/img/cPZD5vPT53_sczzR.jpg>
attachments.text:

text:
A Ray of Hope: Array Programming for the 21st Century

The ideas of APL and its successors, the array programming languages, were two generations ahead of their time. These languages are based on the notion that everything is a tensor, and all operations are rank-polymorphic: they extend automatically to tensors of any rank. These ideas are perfectly suited to an era of machine learning, large scale data, GPUs and other accelerators. Building on recent academic research, we are building ShapeRank, a new statically typed, purely functional language for industrial use, that extends rank-polymorphism to streams. We’ll introduce the key ideas and show how they are realized in ShapeRank.

Presented by Gilad Bracha

<https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=x1FoTYnJxeY>

attachments.title: A Ray of Hope: Array Programming for the 21st Century
attachments.fallback: YouTube Video: A Ray of Hope: Array Programming for the 21st Century

elements.text:
A Ray of Hope: Array Programming for the 21st Century

The ideas of APL and its successors, the array programming languages, were two generations ahead of their time. These languages are based on the notion that everything is a tensor, and all operations are rank-polymorphic: they extend automatically to tensors of any rank. These ideas are perfectly suited to an era of machine learning, large scale data, GPUs and other accelerators. Building on recent academic research, we are building ShapeRank, a new statically typed, purely functional language for industrial use, that extends rank-polymorphism to streams. We’ll introduce the key ideas and show how they are realized in ShapeRank.

Presented by Gilad Bracha

text:
Hi all,
for those who don't yet know, brother and I are working on <https://wasp-lang.dev|Wasp> - a DSL (domain specific language) for building web apps with as little boilerplate as possible. It is open-source and integrates with React/Node.js/Prisma.
Main hypothesis of the experiment: What if web framework was a DSL (we are not the first ones to try this!)?

About half a year ago I posted a prototype here, and now we have released an Alpha! It is still very early, but we have docs, it can be properly installed, and you can build an app (though it still misses many important features).

Goal of the project and our focus is to make developing web apps as easy as possible, while still being flexible enough to be used for real projects in production. In that sense, I think it should be an ok fit for FoC, since it should allow developers who are not very experienced in web development to build a full-stack production-ready web app by themselves. It does add one more layer of abstraction, but the goal is to reduce knowledge barrier.
Bigger, crazier vision is to one day also build a no-code visual editor on top of Wasp, which would allow non-developers to participate alongside developers. We are thinking this could work very good with Wasp since it is declarative, and if we manage to capture big portion of web app functionality in this declarative code, it could be easier to build such editor on top.

When using Wasp, you write code in Wasp (declarative) and Javascript together.
Compiler implemented in Haskell compiles/transpiles the source code of Wasp project into a human-readable JS based web app (which you don't care about unless you decide to eject -> take the code and leave Wasp).

We started working on Wasp about a year ago, first as a side project and then full time (we are bootstraping), and we are excited to see how far we can get with the whole concept! Right now we are trying to get first testers and contributors (actually got our first contributor couple of days ago wohooo!), while we also keep developing Wasp.

Webpage: <https://wasp-lang.dev>
Docs: <https://wasp-lang.dev/docs/>
Todo App tutorial: <https://wasp-lang.dev/docs/tutorials/todo-app> <- probably best place to get started and try it out
Github repo: <https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp>

Thanks for any feedback!

elements.text:
Hi all,
for those who don't yet know, brother and I are working on
Wasp

- a DSL (domain specific language) for building web apps with as little boilerplate as possible. It is open-source and integrates with React/Node.js/Prisma.
Main hypothesis of the experiment: What if web framework was a DSL (we are not the first ones to try this!)?

About half a year ago I posted a prototype here, and now we have released an Alpha! It is still very early, but we have docs, it can be properly installed, and you can build an app (though it still misses many important features).

Goal of the project and our focus is to make developing web apps as easy as possible, while still being flexible enough to be used for real projects in production. In that sense, I think it should be an ok fit for FoC, since it should allow developers who are not very experienced in web development to build a full-stack production-ready web app by themselves. It does add one more layer of abstraction, but the goal is to reduce knowledge barrier.
Bigger, crazier vision is to one day also build a no-code visual editor on top of Wasp, which would allow non-developers to participate alongside developers. We are thinking this could work very good with Wasp since it is declarative, and if we manage to capture big portion of web app functionality in this declarative code, it could be easier to build such editor on top.

When using Wasp, you write code in Wasp (declarative) and Javascript together.
Compiler implemented in Haskell compiles/transpiles the source code of Wasp project into a human-readable JS based web app (which you don't care about unless you decide to eject -> take the code and leave Wasp).

We started working on Wasp about a year ago, first as a side project and then full time (we are bootstraping), and we are excited to see how far we can get with the whole concept! Right now we are trying to get first testers and contributors (actually got our first contributor couple of days ago wohooo!), while we also keep developing Wasp.

(15:00 CT = 21:00 GMT) To join the workshop itself, sign in to Clowdr and join the SPLASH-VI Zoom room at the bottom of the page, where it says “To join the Zoom room to participate in the discussion, click here”. text: I decided to add a second summary graph to the profiler; this one (shown at the top, in purple) gives a view into the time taken on the audio thread - the critical one for good sound processing.  It is shown as continuous here, but really it is discrete; it is somewhat easier to visualise like this I think.  The graph goes more red the closer the audio thread comes to the max time slice it is given to generate audio.  The image here shows a brief bottleneck which I've zoomed into in the bottom of the profiler.  You can see that the yellow 'Audio' region is much bigger for this particular frame, compared to the next ones.  Although I don't have instrumentation for it yet, I believe this is coinciding with the arrival of the first notes into the graph - since a known problem is that some memory allocation is done at this point on the first run (and this particular stress test is playing many notes all at once).  Just something I know I need to get around to fixing. I'm satisfied the profiler is useful enough to leave alone for now, and move onto other things.

elements.text: I decided to add a second summary graph to the profiler; this one (shown at the top, in purple) gives a view into the time taken on the audio thread - the critical one for good sound processing.  It is shown as continuous here, but really it is discrete; it is somewhat easier to visualise like this I think.  The graph goes more red the closer the audio thread comes to the max time slice it is given to generate audio.  The image here shows a brief bottleneck which I've zoomed into in the bottom of the profiler.  You can see that the yellow 'Audio' region is much bigger for this particular frame, compared to the next ones.  Although I don't have instrumentation for it yet, I believe this is coinciding with the arrival of the first notes into the graph - since a known problem is that some memory allocation is done at this point on the first run (and this particular stress test is playing many notes all at once).  Just something I know I need to get around to fixing. I'm satisfied the profiler is useful enough to leave alone for now, and move onto other things.

text:
Funny coincidence to see this today! I’ve been thinking a lot lately about how task specifications decompose into pieces, and how each piece is implemented in a plan. <https://willcrichton.net/expressiveness-benchmark/>

Would be interested to understand more about your plan IR, both the text -> plan and plan -> code translations.

elements.text: Funny coincidence to see this today! I’ve been thinking a lot lately about how task specifications decompose into pieces, and how each piece is implemented in a plan.
Would be interested to understand more about your plan IR, both the text -> plan and plan -> code translations.

text: We didn’t end up with an intermediate plan concept. The system is built around objects (we’re calling them gadgets) that have “parts” (composable bits of state, think components in an ECS) and “processes”

elements.text: We didn’t end up with an intermediate plan concept. The system is built around objects (we’re calling them gadgets) that have “parts” (composable bits of state, think components in an ECS) and “processes”

text: The text in the input is going directly to a process, which has a series of ordered steps like a normal imperative procedure

elements.text: The text in the input is going directly to a process, which has a series of ordered steps like a normal imperative procedure

text: I don't see them as mutually exclusive: we need "thinkers" and "tinkerers" in equal numbers, and the fact is that the intersection between those two groups is larger than it might seem! The corollary to this is that building the future of programming is not a zero-sum game: for someone on Handmade to win doesn't mean anyone in FoC has to lose, and vice-versa. There are always going to be folks who are more willing to engage in building  projects and showing them off, and we provide great platforms to do so in this community, but we have to be conscious that there are just as many folks whose joy lies in the more academic or philosophical pursuits. Sure, these camps are sometimes at odds with each other, but in order to push the needle forward, we need them both!
